content_of_post_to_comment: there's a version of the thing you want to do that you've been holding in your head for a while now. the business, the conversation, the change, the move, the thing you keep circling without landing on. and the reason you keep giving yourself for not starting yet is that you don't quite feel ready. that something is missing, some level of preparation or certainty or confidence that would make the starting feel legitimate instead of premature. the problem is that the feeling you're waiting for doesn't exist before you start. it only exists, if it exists at all, somewhere inside the doing. this isn't the kind of thing that gets easier to hear the more someone repeats it. you already know this version of the advice. you've read it before, probably multiple times, probably agreed with it in the abstract while continuing to wait for the readiness that wasn't coming. knowing it isn't the same as actually stopping the waiting. so instead of repeating the advice, try this. think about the last time you did something that worked out, something you're actually glad you did. and try to remember whether you felt ready before you did it or whether you just did it and the ready feeling showed up later, if at all. because for most things that actually mattered, for most decisions that turned out to be the right ones, the readiness was retrospective. you looked back from the other side of it and said yeah okay that worked, but standing at the beginning of it you mostly just felt uncertain and did it anyway. that's the actual sequence. not ready then start. start then, somewhere in the middle of it, you stop feeling like you're about to fall off something. seven months ago you were in the same place with something else. you remember how long you waited and how little the waiting actually prepared you for anything and how fast the first week of actually doing it moved compared to the months you spent thinking about starting. you don't need a different version of yourself to begin. you just need to go.
